message:
fix(btree/cursor): defragment table-leaf pages on DELETE instead of maintaining a freeblock chain

Previously, `remove_table_cell_from_leaf_deferred` removed the pointer, then
either inserted a freeblock into the sorted/coalesced freeblock chain (if the
freed cell was >= 4 bytes), bumped `fragmented_free_bytes` (if < 4), or
zero-filled the whole usable range (if the page just went empty).

Small mixed INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE churn on table-leaf pages regularly produced
freeblock layouts that SQLite's `btreeComputeFreeSpace()` rejects as "free
space corruption":
  - 1-3 byte gaps between consecutive freeblocks (spec requires > 3).
  - Freeblocks adjacent to `cell_content_offset` that should have been
    merged into the content region.
These layouts only surfaced when a peer SQLite process reopened the DB.

Switch table-leaf DELETE to the same defragment-on-delete strategy that
index-leaf deletes already use:

  1. Remove the pointer from the live `ptrs` vector.
  2. Walk remaining cells sorted by descending on-disk offset and
     `copy_within` each cell into a freshly packed content region at the top
     of the usable page.
  3. Zero the now-dead gap between the pointer-array end and the new
     `cell_content_offset`.
  4. Clear `first_freeblock` and `fragmented_free_bytes`, then set
     `cell_content_offset` to the new packed boundary.

Bounds checks convert overflow / pointer-array overlap into
`FrankenError::DatabaseCorrupt` instead of silent truncation.

Also drop the now-redundant stale-pointer scrub at the end of the function:
the defrag zero-fill already covers the byte range where the old pointer
lived, so the separate 2-byte `fill(0)` is no longer meaningful.

The old freeblock-insert path (`insert_freeblock_sorted_coalesced`) is
retained behind `#[allow(dead_code)]` for future freeblock-chain
experiments, but no DELETE path calls it.

Unrelated correctness touch-up in `BtreeCursorOps::delete`: after the new
`balance_for_delete(cx)?` call clears the cursor stack, fall back to the
seek-by-anchor branch so the cursor lands on the deleted rowid's successor
(or EOF), matching the DELETE cursor contract and the SQLite reference.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
